narrative:
First Warn Forecast & Severe Weather Outlook
Sunday, May 17, 2026
First Warn Headlines
Hot, windy, and increasingly humid conditions continue today across Missouri and Illinois.
Most of today remains dry locally, though isolated storms are possible across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois early this morning.
Severe weather chances increase Monday night into Tuesday across northeast Missouri, central Missouri, the St. Louis metro, southeast Missouri, and west-central Illinois.
Damaging winds, large hail, isolated tornadoes, and torrential rainfall all remain possible early this week.
Cooler and less humid air arrives by midweek behind a slow-moving cold front.

A major severe weather pattern is developing across the central United States, but for today, much of northeast Missouri, central Missouri, the St. Louis metro area, southeast Missouri, and west-central Illinois will remain on the quieter side of the system.

Strong southerly winds will continue transporting warm and humid Gulf air northward today, helping temperatures surge into the upper 80s and lower 90s this afternoon. Wind gusts may frequently reach 25 to 30 mph, especially during the afternoon hours. Aside from a few isolated showers or thunderstorms possible early this morning mainly across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois, much of the region should stay dry through sunset.

While the highest-end severe weather risk today will remain west across Kansas and Nebraska, residents locally should still pay attention to forecast updates tonight into Monday as the atmosphere across Missouri and Illinois becomes increasingly unstable.

Severe Weather Outlook: Monday

Monday will feature another very warm and humid day across the region with highs climbing into the upper 80s. The atmosphere will become increasingly unstable as a slow-moving frontal boundary begins edging closer to Missouri and Illinois.

During the day Monday, scattered showers and thunderstorms may develop across portions of northeast and central Missouri, especially north of I-70. However, confidence remains somewhat lower regarding storm coverage and timing during the daytime hours due to uncertainty involving leftover morning thunderstorms moving in from Kansas and Nebraska.

If clouds and earlier storms clear enough Monday afternoon, the atmosphere could rapidly destabilize once again. This may allow thunderstorms to intensify Monday evening and overnight across:

Northeast Missouri
Central Missouri
The St. Louis metro area
West-central Illinois

The main threats Monday night would include:

Damaging straight-line winds
Large hail
Isolated tornadoes

Storms Monday night may initially develop as discrete supercells farther west before eventually organizing into clusters as they move eastward toward Missouri and Illinois.

At this time, northeast and central Missouri appear to have the highest severe weather potential Monday night locally, though the St. Louis metro and west-central Illinois may also experience strong to severe storms overnight.

Severe Weather Outlook: Tuesday

Tuesday currently appears to hold the greatest widespread severe weather potential across the First Warn viewing area.

A slow-moving cold front is expected to gradually push southeastward through Missouri and Illinois Tuesday afternoon and evening. Ahead of this boundary, the atmosphere will remain extremely warm, humid, and unstable with plenty of fuel available for thunderstorm development.

The greatest concern Tuesday will be along and ahead of the cold front across:

Northeast Missouri
Central Missouri
The St. Louis metro area
Southeast Missouri
West-central Illinois

Depending on the exact timing of the front and how much daytime heating occurs Tuesday afternoon, thunderstorms may rapidly intensify into organized severe storms capable of producing:

Widespread damaging winds
Large hail
Isolated tornadoes
Very heavy rainfall and localized flash flooding

One important factor that will determine Tuesday’s overall threat level is whether storms remain tied directly to the advancing cold front or surge ahead of it. If storms stay more organized along the front during peak heating Tuesday afternoon and evening, the severe weather threat could become more significant locally.

The atmosphere Tuesday will contain:

Strong instability
Rich Gulf moisture
Increasing wind shear
Strong forcing along the cold front

These ingredients support the possibility for multiple rounds of severe thunderstorms somewhere within the region.

At this point:

Northeast and central Missouri may see the earliest severe storm development Tuesday.
The St. Louis metro and west-central Illinois may face stronger storms later Tuesday afternoon and evening.
Southeast Missouri could see storms lingering into Tuesday night as the front slowly shifts southeastward.

Residents across the region should closely monitor forecast updates over the next 24 to 48 hours as confidence in storm timing and placement continues improving.

Behind the cold front, a much cooler and drier air mass arrives Wednesday with highs falling back into the upper 60s and lower 70s across much of Missouri and Illinois.
